questions.question: Explain the difference between electric current and potential difference.
questions.answer: Electric current is the flow of electric charge (electrons) through a conductor, measured in ampere. Potential difference is the work done per unit charge to move charge between two points, measured in volt. Current needs a potential difference to flow.
answer_outline: Flow of charge vs work per charge

questions.question: State Ohm's law and mention the condition under which it is valid.
questions.answer: Ohm's law states V = IR (current is directly proportional to potential difference). It is valid when physical conditions like temperature remain constant (i.e., for ohmic conductors at constant temperature).

questions.question: How is the resistivity of a material related to its resistance? Write the formula.
questions.answer: Resistance R = ρ (l / A), where ρ is resistivity, l is length, and A is cross-sectional area. Resistivity is a material property independent of dimensions.

questions.question: Why do we use copper wires for electrical transmission instead of iron wires?
questions.answer: Copper has very low resistivity, so it offers less resistance to current flow, resulting in lower energy loss as heat. Iron has higher resistivity, leading to more heat loss.

questions.question: Two resistors of 3 Ω and 6 Ω are connected in series. Find the equivalent resistance.
questions.answer: In series, R_eq = R1 + R2 = 3 + 6 = 9 Ω.

questions.question: Three resistors each of 2 Ω are connected in parallel. Calculate the equivalent resistance.
Explain why the current is the same in every part of a series circuit.
questions.answer: In a series circuit, there is only one path for the flow of current. Charge cannot accumulate, so the same current flows through all components.

questions.question: Why does the potential difference get divided across resistors in a series combination?
questions.answer: In series, the total voltage supplied by the battery is shared among the resistors in proportion to their resistances (V1 = IR1, V2 = IR2, etc.), since current is the same.

questions.question: State Joule's law of heating and write its mathematical expression.
questions.answer: The heat produced in a conductor is directly proportional to the square of current, resistance of the conductor, and time for which current flows. H = I²Rt.

questions.question: Why are household electrical appliances connected in parallel instead of series?
questions.answer: In parallel, each appliance gets the full rated voltage (220 V), can be operated independently, and if one fails, others continue working. In series, voltage divides and failure of one stops all.

questions.question: An electric iron draws 5 A current at 220 V. Calculate its power.
questions.answer: Power P = V × I = 220 × 5 = 1100 W or 1.1 kW.

questions.question: How is electrical energy consumption calculated in kWh? Give the relation with joules.
questions.answer: Energy (in kWh) = Power (in kW) × Time (in hours). 1 kWh = 3.6 × 10⁶ J (since 1 kW = 1000 W and 1 h = 3600 s).

questions.question: Why is a fuse wire made of a material with low melting point?
questions.answer: During overload or short circuit, high current flows, producing large heat (I²R). Low melting point wire melts quickly, breaking the circuit and preventing fire or damage.

questions.question: A 100 W bulb is operated for 10 hours. Calculate the electrical energy consumed.
questions.answer: Energy = Power × Time = 100 W × 10 h = 1000 Wh = 1 kWh.

questions.question: Explain why the resistance of most metals increases with temperature.
questions.answer: As temperature rises, metal atoms vibrate more, increasing collisions with free electrons, which obstructs their flow, thereby increasing resistance.

questions.question: What is the advantage of connecting resistors in parallel in a circuit?
questions.answer: Parallel connection decreases equivalent resistance (more paths for current), allowing higher total current for the same voltage. Each branch gets full voltage.

questions.question: Calculate the heat generated when 3 A current passes through a 10 Ω resistor for 2 minutes.
Why do electric bulbs have a coiled filament?
questions.answer: Coiling increases the length of the filament in a small space, increasing resistance and heat production, which makes the filament glow brighter without needing very high voltage.

questions.question: Differentiate between ohmic and non-ohmic conductors with one example each.
questions.answer: Ohmic conductors obey Ohm's law (V-I graph straight line), e.g., copper wire at constant temperature. Non-ohmic do not (curved graph), e.g., filament lamp (resistance increases with temperature).

questions.question: A heater rated 1000 W is used on 250 V supply. Find the resistance of its coil.
Why are alloys like nichrome used in heating elements instead of pure metals?
questions.answer: Nichrome has high resistivity and high melting point, produces more heat for given current, and does not oxidise easily at high temperatures.

questions.question: Explain how an ammeter is connected in a circuit and why it has very low resistance.
questions.answer: Ammeter is connected in series with the circuit. It has very low resistance so that it does not significantly reduce the current or alter the circuit's behaviour.

questions.question: A current of 2 A flows through two resistors of 5 Ω each connected in parallel. Find the total power dissipated.
questions.answer: Current divides equally: 1 A each. Power per resistor = I²R = 1 × 5 = 5 W. Total power = 5 + 5 = 10 W. (Alternatively: R_eq = 2.5 Ω, P = I²R_eq = 4 × 2.5 = 10 W).

questions.question: Why does the resistance of a conductor decrease when its cross-sectional area is increased?
questions.answer: Larger cross-sectional area provides more space for free electrons to flow, reducing collisions and opposition to current, hence resistance decreases (R ∝ 1/A).

questions.question: A 40 W fluorescent lamp is used for 6 hours daily. Calculate the monthly energy consumption (30 days) in kWh and the cost if 1 unit costs ₹6.
questions.answer: Daily energy = 40 W × 6 h = 240 Wh = 0.24 kWh. Monthly = 0.24 × 30 = 7.2 kWh. Cost = 7.2 × 6 = ₹43.2.
